questions.text: The current constitution of Ethiopia was adopted in which year?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia (FDRE) was adopted on August 21, 1995. It establishes Ethiopia as a federal state with a parliamentary system of government.

questions.text: Which branch of government in Ethiopia is responsible for making federal laws?
options: A) The Executive (Prime Minister and Cabinet)
B) The Federal Parliament (HPR and HoF)
C) The Federal Supreme Court
D) The President
questions.explanation: The Federal Parliament of Ethiopia consists of two chambers: the House of Peoples' Representatives (HPR) and the House of Federation (HoF). Together they are the legislative branch responsible for making federal laws.

questions.text: According to the FDRE Constitution, sovereignty in Ethiopia belongs to:
options: A) The Prime Minister
B) The Parliament
C) The Nations, Nationalities, and Peoples of Ethiopia
D) The ruling political party
questions.explanation: Article 8 of the FDRE Constitution states: 'All sovereign power resides in the Nations, Nationalities and Peoples of Ethiopia.' This is the principle of popular sovereignty.

questions.text: Which of the following is a fundamental duty of Ethiopian citizens under the constitution?
options: A) To pay taxes and defend the country
B) To join the ruling political party
C) To work only in the government sector
D) To attend parliament sessions
questions.explanation: The FDRE Constitution outlines duties of citizens including: paying taxes, protecting the environment, defending the country, and respecting the rights of others. Paying taxes and national defense are core duties.

questions.text: The rule of law means that:
options: A) Only rulers and officials are above the law
B) Every person and institution, including the government, is subject to and accountable to the law
C) Laws can be changed by any citizen at any time
D) Military force determines what is legal
questions.explanation: The rule of law is the principle that all people and institutions -- including government officials -- are subject to and accountable under the law. No one is above the law.

questions.text: Human rights are best described as rights that:
options: A) Are granted by governments and can be taken away
B) Apply only to citizens of a specific country
C) Are inherent to all human beings regardless of nationality, race, gender, or religion
D) Only wealthy and educated people can claim
questions.explanation: Human rights are universal, inherent, inalienable, and indivisible rights that belong to all human beings by virtue of their humanity -- not granted by governments. They are enshrined in the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

questions.text: Ethiopia's federal system divides power between the federal government and:
options: A) Districts (woredas)
B) Regional states
C) Village councils
D) International organizations
questions.explanation: Ethiopia has a federal system where power is constitutionally divided between the federal (central) government and the regional states (currently 12 regions). Regions have their own governments and councils.

questions.text: Freedom of expression, as a fundamental right, primarily protects the right to:
options: A) Say anything without any legal consequences whatsoever
B) Express opinions, ideas, and information without undue government interference
C) Broadcast television programs without a license
D) Criticize individuals in private conversations only
questions.explanation: Freedom of expression protects the right to share opinions, information, and ideas without government censorship or punishment. However, it is not absolute -- it may be limited by laws against incitement, defamation, and hate speech.

options: A) Opt 1
B) Opt 2
C) Opt 3
D) Opt 4
